attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the antenatal clinic for her 20-week anomaly scan in her second pregnancy. She has no significant medical history apart from her previous pregnancy two years ago, which resulted in an uncomplicated vaginal delivery at term. She chose not to have first trimester screening for chromosomal abnormalities in her current pregnancy. Her blood group is O Rhesus negative, and she had no known antibodies in her previous pregnancy. During her previous pregnancy, she declined anti-D immunoglobulin administration antenatally and postnatally. She does not take any regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. An ultrasound scan reveals fetal hydrops with generalised skin oedema, ascites, and pleural effusion. The middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity is increased.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
In this scenario, the most likely diagnosis is Rhesus isoimmunisation. Several factors point to this diagnosis:

1. The mother is Rhesus negative, and she declined anti-D immunoglobulin after her first delivery, which would have prevented sensitisation.

2. The current pregnancy shows signs of fetal hydrops on ultrasound.

3. The increased middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity suggests fetal anaemia, which is characteristic of Rhesus isoimmunisation.

Parvovirus B19 infection can cause fetal hydrops, but it typically occurs in the second trimester of the first affected pregnancy. The patient has also not reported any history of illness making this a less likely diagnosis.

A chromosomal abnormality could potentially cause hydrops, but it would be less likely than rhesus isoimmunisation in this case.

A congenital heart defect can lead to hydrops but would not explain the increased middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity suggesting anaemia.

CMV infection can cause congenital abnormalities and, in severe cases, hydrops fetalis. The patient has also not reported any history of illness making this a less likely diagnosis.

Rhesus isoimmunisation occurs when a Rhesus negative mother develops antibodies against Rhesus positive fetal blood cells. The risk increases with each subsequent pregnancy if sensitisation has occurred. In this case, the refusal of anti-D prophylaxis after the first pregnancy significantly increased the risk of sensitisation. The antibodies can cross the placenta and cause haemolysis of fetal red blood cells, leading to anaemia and potentially hydrops fetalis.

Management of this condition typically involves close monitoring of antibody titres, regular ultrasounds to assess for signs of fetal anaemia, and potentially intrauterine blood transfusions if severe anaemia develops. The prognosis depends on the severity of the condition and how early it is detected and managed.
learningPoints: Rhesus isoimmunisation can cause fetal hydrops, particularly in subsequent pregnancies of sensitised Rhesus negative mothers.
Refusal of anti-D prophylaxis significantly increases the risk of Rhesus isoimmunisation in future pregnancies.
Early detection and monitoring of anti-D antibodies is crucial in managing pregnancies at risk of Rhesus isoimmunisation.
references: Costumbrado J, Mansour T, Ghassemzadeh S. Rh Incompatibility. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 [updated 2024 May 7]. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK574830/
